articles.title: Japanese Prime Minister's Rock Fandom and Cultural Diplomacy
articles.summary: Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, a long-time enthusiast of hard rock and heavy metal, met the British band Deep Purple in Tokyo. As a super fan since primary school, Takaichi played in a Deep Purple tribute band as a youth and continues to drum, even using their music during personal moments. During the meeting, she expressed deep respect, calling the band her 'god,' and gifted signed Japanese-made drumsticks to drummer Ian Paice. Deep Purple is touring Japan, where they recorded a famous live album in 1972, and Takaichi hopes the tour will excite fans and foster cultural exchange between the UK and Japan. The band appreciated the visit as an 'added bonus' to their trip. This event highlights Takaichi's personal passions amid her political role, as she faces challenges like diplomatic tensions and economic issues. Formed in 1968, Deep Purple is a key figure in heavy metal history, known for hits like 'Smoke on the Water.'

articles.title: Great Britain Advances to Billie Jean King Cup Finals with Upset Victory
articles.summary: Great Britain secured a spot in the Billie Jean King Cup finals by defeating Australia 3-1 in a qualifying tie, highlighted by a crucial doubles win. Harriet Dart and Jodie Burrage, partnering for the first time, beat Storm Hunter and Ellen Perez 6-3, 6-4 in straight sets on hard courts in Melbourne, clinching an unassailable 3-0 lead. This victory built on earlier singles successes: 17-year-old Mika Stojsavljevic stunned higher-ranked Talia Gibson on her debut, and Dart overcame Kimberly Birrell after dropping the first set. Despite a dead-rubber loss by Katie Swan to Emerson Jones, the team celebrated their qualification. Captain Anne Keothavong expressed confidence in winning the title, praising the players' execution and nerve. Great Britain achieved this as underdogs, missing four top-100 ranked players, demonstrating depth and resilience. They join hosts China in the eight-team finals in Shenzhen, while Australia heads to play-offs.

articles.title: Sinner Advances to Monte Carlo Semi-Final with Victory Over Auger-Aliassime
articles.summary: Jannik Sinner, the Italian second seed, secured a straight-sets victory over Felix Auger-Aliassime with scores of 6-3, 6-4 in the quarter-finals of the Monte Carlo Masters. This win extends his impressive winning streak in Masters 1000 events to 20 matches, a run that includes recent titles at Paris, Indian Wells, and Miami. Sinner expressed satisfaction with his performance, noting improvements in his game despite acknowledging that his serve needs work. The victory sets up a semi-final clash with Alexander Zverev, who advanced after a tough three-set win against Joao Fonseca. Meanwhile, reigning champion Carlos Alcaraz, the top seed, also progressed to the semi-finals with a dominant win over Alexander Bublik, and he will face unseeded Valentin Vacherot, who upset fifth seed Alex de Minaur. Sinner has the opportunity to replace Alcaraz as world number one in the upcoming rankings if he wins the tournament, adding stakes to the competition. The article highlights key matches and player performances, emphasizing the competitive nature of the event.

articles.title: Leadership Change at Dolce & Gabbana Amid Financial Struggles
articles.summary: Stefano Gabbana, co-founder of the luxury fashion house Dolce & Gabbana, has resigned as chair of the company, which he established with Domenico Dolce in 1985. He will retain his creative role, continuing to design collections alongside Dolce. The company is facing significant financial challenges, including approximately €450 million in debt, attributed to a downturn in the luxury retail sector, particularly reduced spending in China. Alfonso Dolce, Domenico's brother and the firm's chief executive, assumed the chair position on January 1. Dolce & Gabbana is diversifying into new markets like hospitality and furniture, but experts highlight ongoing debt negotiations with creditors. Fashion analyst Priya Raj notes that while the brand has maintained popularity despite controversies, such as criticism over model diversity, its financial management requires external assistance, possibly through a minority investor or strategic partnership to regain stability.

articles.title: How Imperfections Boost Perovskite Solar Cell Efficiency
articles.summary: Lead-halide perovskites, a class of materials with structural flaws, are emerging as efficient and low-cost alternatives to silicon solar cells. Researchers at the Institute of Science and Technology Austria (ISTA) have published a study in Nature Communications explaining this phenomenon. Unlike silicon, which requires high purity for optimal performance, perovskites benefit from their imperfections. The team, including Dmytro Rak and Zhanybek Alpichshev, discovered that a network of defects allows electrical charges to travel long distances, enhancing energy conversion. They used nonlinear optical techniques to show that internal forces within perovskites actively separate electrons and holes, preventing recombination and enabling efficient charge transport. This insight addresses a long-standing puzzle in materials science and could accelerate the commercialization of perovskite solar cells. Perovskites, first identified in the 1970s, gained attention in the 2010s for their light-conversion abilities and are now used in LEDs and X-ray detection. The study highlights how solution-based manufacturing methods make perovskites affordable while maintaining performance comparable to silicon, offering potential for sustainable energy solutions.

articles.title: Innovative Chip Design to Reduce Data Center Energy Consumption
articles.summary: Researchers at the University of California San Diego have developed a new chip design aimed at cutting energy waste in data centers, which are critical for supporting increasing digital activities like streaming and cloud computing. The chip focuses on improving DC-DC step-down converters, components that reduce high voltage electricity to lower levels needed by hardware such as GPU processors. In data centers, electricity is often supplied at 48 volts, but GPUs require only 1-5 volts, creating a large voltage drop that traditional converters handle inefficiently. The new design uses piezoelectric resonators, which store and transfer energy through mechanical vibrations instead of magnetic fields like conventional inductors. This approach allows for a hybrid converter that combines piezoelectric elements with capacitors, tested in a prototype chip. Results showed it could convert 48 volts to 4.8 volts with 96.2% peak efficiency and deliver higher output current than previous designs. Published in Nature Communications, this innovation could lead to more compact and energy-efficient systems, addressing the growing power demands of advanced computing.
